school: Umayyad general — crossed the Strait of Gibraltar in 711 with 7,000 men; conquered the Visigoth kingdom; Gibraltar is named after him (Jabal al-Tariq)

books.title: The Gibraltar Speech (attributed)
books.url: ,note:"'The sea is behind you, the enemy before you' — the most famous speech in Islamic military history

quotes: The sea is behind you and the enemy is before you — where will you flee?
Know that I am the first to do what I command you to do and the first to forbid what I forbid myself.
Victory comes not from numbers but from certainty.

school: Umayyad general — conquered Sind (modern Pakistan) at age 17; opened South Asia to Islam; one of the most remarkable military careers in history

books.title: Chach Nama
books.url: ,note:"Account of the conquest of Sind — one of the first Islamic histories of South Asia

quotes: Justice is the first weapon of the conqueror — without it, he has only taken land, not hearts.
The people must feel safe — an army that terrorises its own subjects has already lost.
Administer what God has given you as a trust, not as a possession.

school: Zaidi School of Islamic Jurisprudence — founder; great-grandson of the Prophet

books.title: Musnad Zayd ibn Ali
books.note: Founder of Zaidi school; his Musnad is the oldest surviving fiqh collection in Shia Islam

school: Hanafi School — Founder. Largest madhab in the world (~45% of Muslims).

books.title: Kitab al-Athar (The Book of Narrations)
books.note: Oldest surviving fiqh work by a named author.

quotes: Opinion is the use of reason in the absence of divine text.
The opinion is only my opinion — if someone brings me something better, I will take it.
Whoever seeks knowledge for the sake of the Hereafter gains much; whoever seeks it for the sake of the world gains little.
The greatest jurisprudence is the knowledge of the self — what serves it and what harms it.
Whoever uses analogy without the Sunnah will go astray.
The scholar must fear God in his knowledge just as the ruler must fear God in his power.

school: Founder of the Syrian (Awza'i) school of jurisprudence, which dominated the Islamic west for two centuries before being superseded by Malikism; mufti of the military frontier towns; famously debated with Abu Yusuf (Hanafi) over legal methodology; his school was influential in Andalus; died in Beirut which has a district named after him

books.title: al-Masa'il fi'l-Fiqh (Legal Questions)
books.note: Foundation of the Awza'i legal school

quotes: Hold fast to the traces of those who came before you, even if the people reject you.
The one who follows the Sunnah is never truly alone — even if he stands by himself.
Follow the traces of those who have gone before you, even if the people abandon you.
No people have abandoned a Sunnah except that God afflicted them with what is worse.
The sign of a scholar is that he speaks little and listens much.

school: Maliki School — Founder. Dominant in North Africa, West Africa, and Andalusia.

books.title: Al-Muwatta (The Well-Trodden Path)
books.note: Oldest surviving complete book of Islamic law and hadith — predates al-Bukhari.

parents: Anas bin Abi Aans (Malik) / Aaliyah bint Shurayk al-Azdiyya
quotes: The last of this community will not be reformed except by what reformed its first.
The Sunnah is like Noah's Ark — whoever boards it is saved, and whoever stays behind is drowned.
Do not say of any matter, 'I will do this', unless you also say, 'if Allah wills'.
The last of this community will not be rectified except by what rectified its first.
None of you should say: 'My opinion is correct but possibly wrong.' Say instead: 'This is what I believe, and God knows best.'
Knowledge is a light that God places wherever He wills — it is not merely the quantity of narrations.

school: The leading scholar of Egypt and founder of an independent legal school; al-Shafi'i said: 'al-Layth is more learned in jurisprudence than Malik, but his followers were negligent in transmitting his opinions'; known for extraordinary generosity — he reportedly spent 120,000 dinars in charity in a single year; his school was eventually absorbed by Malikism in Egypt

books.title: Legal opinions collected in al-Mudawwana chains
books.note: His legal opinions were absorbed into the Maliki school — a major scholarly tragedy

quotes: Malik’s rulings are sound for the people of Medina — but they do not always suit the people of Egypt.
The scholar who does not adapt his knowledge to his context has not yet mastered it.
Jurisprudence is not one size — it fits the people it serves.
Malik’s rulings are sound for the people of Medina — but they do not always suit the people of Egypt.
The scholar who does not adapt his knowledge to his context has not yet mastered it.
Jurisprudence is not one size — it fits the people it serves.

school: Called 'the Commander of the Faithful in Hadith' for his unparalleled mastery of hadith criticism; founder of an independent legal school (later absorbed into Hanbalism); fled authorities to avoid being forced to serve; a fierce critic of Umayyad and Abbasid rulers; his asceticism and scholarship are legendary — 'Whoever Allah wishes good for, He makes him like Sufyan al-Thawri'

books.title: al-Jami' (Hadith Collection)
books.note: Earliest Kufan hadith compilation — most transmitted through secondary sources

parents: Sa'id bin Masrooq Ath-Thawri / A pious woman
quotes: We sought knowledge for reasons other than God — but knowledge refused to be sought for anything less.
If you see a scholar who loves the world, do not trust him with your religion.

school: An eighth-century Persian scholar and translator who played a pivotal role in transmitting Persian and Indian literary traditions into Arabic. His translation of the Panchatantra into Arabic as Kalila wa-Dimna became one of the most widely read works of Arabic prose literature. He also translated important Sasanian works on statecraft and governance. He was executed around 756 CE, reportedly on charges of heresy, though political motivations are widely suspected.
quotes: The intelligent man learns from the experience of others — the fool learns only from his own.
A king who has no wise counsellor is like an eye without sight.
Speak the truth to the powerful even when they hate it — silence in that moment is betrayal.

school: Primary student of Imam Abu Hanifa who codified Hanafi administrative and constitutional law; appointed first Chief Judge (Qadi al-Quzat) of the Abbasid Caliphate by Harun al-Rashid; his Kitab al-Kharaj is the foundational text of Islamic fiscal and land law

books.title: Kitab al-Kharaj
books.note: Foundational text of Islamic fiscal and land law, written for Harun al-Rashid

quotes: The ruler has no right to the people’s wealth except what the law specifies — and the law is God’s, not the ruler’s.
Taxation without justice is confiscation.
The judge who fears the powerful and pities the weak has understood nothing about justice.

school: The most authoritative student of Imam Malik; his Al-Mudawwana (compiled by Sahnun from his transmissions) became the foundational text of Maliki law; spent 20 years in Imam Malik's circle and transmitted the vast majority of the Muwatta rulings that formed the Maliki school in Egypt and North Africa

books.title: Al-Mudawwana al-Kubra (via Sahnun)
books.note: Foundational Maliki legal compendium compiled from Ibn al-Qasim's transmissions of Imam Malik's opinions

parents: al-Qasim ibn Muhammad / Fatima bint al-Mundhir bin al-Zubayr

school: Student of both Abu Hanifa and Imam Malik; systematised the Hanafi legal corpus into the authoritative Zahir al-Riwaya (Six Books); his Siyar al-Kabir is the earliest and most comprehensive treatise on Islamic international law governing relations between Muslim and non-Muslim states

books.title: Al-Siyar al-Kabir
books.note: Earliest comprehensive treatise on Islamic international law

books.title: Al-Jami al-Saghir
books.note: Part of the canonical Zahir al-Riwaya — six books systematising Hanafi law

quotes: International relations between nations must be governed by principles — not merely by power.
The law of nations is not invented by rulers — it is discovered by scholars.
Even in war there are rules — and whoever abandons them has abandoned civilisation.

school: Shafi'i School — Founder. Dominant in SE Asia, East Africa, and the Levant.

books.title: Al-Risala (The Letter — founding text of Islamic legal theory)
books.note: Founded the science of Usul al-Fiqh (principles of Islamic jurisprudence).

books.title: Kitab al-Umm (The Mother Book — comprehensive fiqh, 8 vols)
Al-Risala (Foundations of Islamic Jurisprudence)
books.note: Founding text of Islamic legal theory — English

quotes: Knowledge is not what is memorised. Knowledge is what benefits.
Knowledge is not what is memorised — knowledge is what benefits.
Whoever wishes to know the world should study history; whoever wishes to know himself should study jurisprudence.
Whoever wishes that God open the light of his heart must leave what does not concern him, abandon sin, and shun disputes.
The more I learn, the more I discover how much I do not know.
If you find that my opinion contradicts the hadith of the Prophet, follow the hadith and throw my opinion against the wall.

school: Muʿtazila
quotes: Whoever knows himself knows his Lord.

school: Compiled Al-Mudawwana al-Kubra from Ibn al-Qasim's transmissions of Imam Malik — the text that defined Maliki jurisprudence for North Africa, Spain, and West Africa for a millennium; served as Chief Judge (Qadi) of Kairouan and established it as a centre of Maliki learning, directly influencing the Timbuktu tradition

books.title: Al-Mudawwana al-Kubra
books.note: The canonical compendium of Maliki law — basis for legal practice across North and West Africa

school: Hanbali School — Founder. Dominant in Arabian Peninsula. Symbol of Sunni orthodoxy against state theology.

books.title: Musnad Ahmad (~30,000 hadith — largest single collection)
books.note: Largest single hadith collection by one scholar.

parents: Muhammad Ibn Hanbal / Saffiyah bint Maimoonah bint 'Abdul Malik
quotes: Patience is to faith what the head is to the body.
If you see a man who loves to debate and argue, avoid him — for he has been afflicted.
The Sunnah in my view is to hold fast to what the companions of the Messenger were upon.
If you want God to be with you wherever you are, be with God wherever He commands.
The foundation of the Sunnah in our view is holding fast to what the Companions of the Messenger of God were upon.
Whoever is patient with the difficulties of this life will inherit the ease of the next.

school: Shafi'i School — al-Shafi'i's most distinguished student; his Mukhtasar is the foundational Shafi'i legal text

books.title: Mukhtasar al-Muzani (Compendium of Shafi'i Law)
books.note: Standard Shafi'i legal text used in madrasas for centuries — al-Shafi'i said 'al-Muzani is the champion of my school'

school: A ninth-century Persian geographer and bureaucrat who served as the Abbasid postmaster of Jibal province, he authored Kitab al-Masalik wa-l-Mamalik (Book of Roads and Kingdoms), one of the earliest surviving Arabic geographical works. The book catalogued the postal routes, provinces, and tax revenues of the Abbasid caliphate, serving as both a geographical reference and an administrative handbook. His work also contains some of the earliest Arabic references to China, Korea, and Japan.

A ninth-century Persian historian who lived in Baghdad, he authored two major historical works: Futuh al-Buldan (Conquests of the Lands) and Ansab al-Ashraf (Genealogies of the Nobles). His Futuh al-Buldan remains one of the most important primary sources for the early Muslim conquests and the administrative organisation of the early caliphate. He served at the Abbasid court and was known for his meticulous approach to historical documentation.
quotes: History is the record of what people leave behind, not what they carry with them.

school: Baghdadi / Independent Jariri school — Founded the science of historical narrative (isnad-based history)

books.title: Tarikh al-Umam wal-Muluk (History of Prophets and Kings — 40 vols)
books.note: Most comprehensive early history of the Islamic world — the foundational source for all Islamic history.

books.title: Jami al-Bayan fi Tafsir al-Quran (The Great Tafsir — 30 vols)
History of al-Tabari Vol.1
books.note: SUNY multi-volume translation of Tarikh al-Tabari — English

quotes: Every day of your life is a page of your history.
I have compiled this history so that those who come after may learn from those who came before.
Knowledge is a trust — the one who carries it must carry it faithfully.
The one who learns history learns the fate of nations — and the one who ignores it shares their fate.
I have collected in this book what the scholars have said — and I leave the judgment to the reader.

school: Hanafi School — switched from Shafi'i to Hanafi under his maternal uncle Al-Muzani; his Al-Aqida al-Tahawiyya is the most universally accepted creedal text in Sunni Islam

books.title: Al-Aqidah al-Tahawiyyah (The Creed of Imam al-Tahawi)
The Creed of Imam al-Tahawi (al-Aqida al-Tahawiyya)
books.note: Classical Sunni creed — English

school: A ninth-century historian and geographer of Abbasid Baghdad, he authored Tarikh al-Yaqubi (History of al-Yaqubi), a universal history from creation to his own time, and Kitab al-Buldan (Book of Countries). His historical work is notable for its attention to non-Arab and pre-Islamic civilisations, reflecting his broad intellectual curiosity. He travelled widely across the Islamic world and his geographical writings draw on firsthand observation.

books.title: Tarikh al-Yaqubi (History of al-Yaqubi)
books.note: 9th-century universal history — the source of our early Islamic data extraction project.

school: A tenth-century Persian geographer who authored Kitab al-Masalik wa-l-Mamalik (Book of Roads and Kingdoms), one of the foundational works of the Balkhi school of Islamic geography. His work built upon the earlier geographical tradition of al-Balkhi and included detailed maps of the Islamic world's provinces. His systematic approach to regional description influenced subsequent geographers including Ibn Hawqal.
Maturidi School of Kalam — Founder. Paired with Ash'ari as the two pillars of Sunni theology. Dominant among Hanafi Muslims.

books.title: Kitab al-Tawhid (Book of Divine Unity)
books.note: The foundational text of Maturidi theology.

quotes: Reason and revelation are complementary — neither contradicts the other when properly understood.

school: Ash'ari School — Founder. The dominant theological school of Sunni Islam worldwide.

books.title: Maqalat al-Islamiyyin (Survey of Islamic Theological Positions)

quotes: We affirm what God has affirmed for Himself, and deny what He has denied, without asking how.
I hold what the pious ancestors held — but I defend it with the tools of reason.
The Quran is the speech of God — uncreated in its essence, carried in created letters.
Between pure rationalism and pure literalism lies the straight path.

school: A tenth-century Abbasid-era scholar, chess master, and courtier, he was renowned as one of the greatest chess players of the medieval Islamic world. He served as tutor and companion to several Abbasid caliphs and compiled important anthologies of Arabic poetry. His works on the reigns of the caliphs he served provide valuable primary source material for Abbasid court history.

A tenth-century Yemeni polymath, he excelled in geography, history, astronomy, and poetry. His major work Sifat Jazirat al-Arab (Description of the Arabian Peninsula) is the most comprehensive medieval geographical account of Arabia, and his Iklil (The Crown) preserves the genealogies and history of South Arabian peoples. He is considered one of the foremost scholars of Yemeni heritage and Arabian geography.
Mu'tazili / Universal Scholarship — First to integrate geography, natural history and world history

books.title: Muruj al-Dhahab (Meadows of Gold — encyclopaedic world history)
books.note: Combines world history, natural history, philosophy and geography — the Islamic world's first great encyclopaedic history.

quotes: The wise man is he who knows himself.

school: A tenth-century Arab scholar and litterateur based in Baghdad, he is best known for his monumental work Kitab al-Aghani (Book of Songs), a multi-volume encyclopaedia of Arabic poetry, music, and literary history. The work preserves invaluable information about pre-Islamic and early Islamic culture, poets, and musicians that would otherwise have been lost. He spent over fifty years compiling the work, drawing on both written sources and oral traditions.
quotes: Poetry is the archive of the Arabs — through it their wisdom, their wars, and their loves are preserved.
The singer gives the poem a second life — music is the soul that text alone cannot carry.
I collected what would have been lost — that is a scholar’s most urgent duty.

school: A tenth-century Baghdadi bibliographer and bookseller, he compiled al-Fihrist (The Catalogue), an extraordinary index of all books written in Arabic up to his time. The work catalogues thousands of titles across every field of knowledge, from theology and philosophy to natural sciences and literature, providing an unparalleled snapshot of Islamic intellectual life. His Fihrist remains an indispensable reference for understanding the scope and breadth of medieval Arabic scholarship.
Maliki school — leading Maliki jurist of North Africa; wrote the first treatise on Islamic education

books.title: Al-Risala al-Mufassala
books.url: ,note:"On the Conditions of Teachers — first Islamic treatise on education

quotes: The child who is not taught the Quran has been deprived of the greatest gift.
Education is not the filling of a vessel — it is the lighting of a fire.
The teacher’s responsibility is the heaviest in any society — for he shapes what all others will become.

school: A tenth-century Arab geographer and traveller, he authored Surat al-Ard (Face of the Earth), a comprehensive geographical account of the Islamic world based on decades of personal travel. He revised and expanded the earlier work of al-Istakhri, adding detailed observations of trade routes, economies, and populations. His accounts of Sicily, North Africa, and the Saharan trade routes are among the most detailed surviving from the medieval period.

A tenth-century Palestinian geographer who authored Ahsan al-Taqasim fi Ma'rifat al-Aqalim (The Best Divisions for Knowledge of the Regions), widely regarded as the finest geographical work of the medieval Islamic world. Unlike many predecessors, he travelled extensively throughout the Islamic lands he described, bringing personal observation and critical analysis to his accounts. His work combined physical geography with detailed information about local economies, customs, and dialects.
quotes: I have visited every region of the earth, and I have found no people more generous than the people of the Levant.

school: Baghdad School of Shia Theology
Asharite Kalam
quotes: The rational mind is the first obligation — for without it, no religious duty can be fulfilled.

school: A tenth-century Persian-born Arabic prose writer, he is credited with inventing the maqama literary genre, a form of rhetorical fiction combining narrative prose with poetry. His Maqamat al-Hamadhani featured the adventures of a witty rogue named Abu al-Fath al-Iskandari, told through an unreliable narrator. This literary innovation profoundly influenced Arabic literature and inspired al-Hariri's celebrated maqamat a century later.

An eleventh-century Shafi'i jurist and political theorist based in Baghdad, he authored al-Ahkam al-Sultaniyyah (The Ordinances of Government), one of the most influential treatises on Islamic constitutional law and political theory. The work systematically defined the qualifications, duties, and limits of the caliph's authority, as well as the roles of judges, governors, and other officials. He served as a chief judge and diplomatic envoy for the Abbasid caliphs and his legal framework shaped Sunni political thought for centuries.

books.title: The Ordinances of Government (al-Ahkam al-Sultaniyya)
books.note: Classical Islamic constitutional law — English

quotes: Justice is the foundation of sovereignty.
Leadership is established to succeed the prophethood in the protection of the faith and the management of worldly affairs.
Justice is the foundation of sovereignty — the ruler who abandons it has abandoned the purpose of his rule.
Leadership is established to protect the faith and manage worldly affairs.
Justice is the foundation of sovereignty — the ruler who abandons it has abandoned his purpose.

school: Zahiri School — Leader. His literalism opposed both Ash'ari theology and analogical jurisprudence.

books.title: Tawq al-Hamama (The Ring of the Dove — on love)
books.note: Most beautiful Arabic treatise on love — a medieval masterpiece of philosophy, poetry and autobiography.

books.title: The Ring of the Dove (Tawq al-Hamama)
books.note: Famous treatise on love and lovers in Andalusia — English

quotes: Every soul pleased with itself is ignoble; every soul displeased with itself is noble.
Whoever points out your faults to you has given you a great gift.
Time is not gold — time is life itself.

school: Hanafi School
quotes: The foundations of jurisprudence are four: the Book, the Sunnah, consensus, and analogy — and all four are one light.
A ruling built on hardship is not a ruling — it is a burden, and burdens are not the way of this religion.

school: An eleventh-century Andalusian geographer and historian, he authored Kitab al-Masalik wa-l-Mamalik (Book of Roads and Kingdoms), one of the most important geographical works of medieval Islam. Notably, he never travelled outside the Iberian Peninsula, composing his geographical accounts entirely from literary sources and travellers' reports. His works provide some of the earliest detailed descriptions of West Africa and the Ghana Empire.
Ash'ari / Shafi'i — Greatest theologian before al-Ghazali; taught in Mecca and Medina for years

books.title: Al-Waraqat (The Pages — usul al-fiqh primer)
books.note: Most widely taught text in Islamic legal methodology — studied in every traditional madrasa today.

quotes: The purpose of theology is to know God as He truly is.
Knowledge is not the abundance of narrations — knowledge is a light that God places in the heart.
The one who masters the foundations needs no authority but his own understanding.
The scholar who has not mastered disagreement has not mastered agreement — for he does not know what he holds or why.
Certainty is reached by evidence — whoever claims certainty without evidence has only claimed it.
The purpose of theology is not victory in debate — it is the protection of faith.

school: Turkic Linguistic School — compiled the first comprehensive dictionary of Turkic languages

books.title: Diwan Lughat al-Turk (Compendium of Turkic Languages)
books.note: First comprehensive Turkic language dictionary and encyclopedia — essential source for Turkic history and linguistics

school: Shafi'i / Khorasan School — leading Shafi'i jurist; his Tafsir is an authoritative Quranic commentary

books.title: Masabih al-Sunnah (Lamps of the Sunnah — hadith compilation)
books.note: His Tafsir al-Baghawi and Masabih al-Sunnah are authoritative references in Sunni scholarship

school: Almohad movement founder — studied in Baghdad; returned to Morocco and founded the Almohad dynasty based on strict tawhid

books.title: A'azz ma Yutlab
books.url: ,note:"Almohad creed — foundational text of the Almohad reform movement

school: Maliki — Most celebrated Maliki scholar of medieval al-Andalus and the Maghrib

books.title: Al-Shifa bi-Ta'rif Huquq al-Mustafa (The Cure — on the Rights of the Prophet)
books.note: Most beloved book about Prophet Muhammad ever written — still read daily across the Muslim world.

books.title: Muhammad: Messenger of Allah (al-Shifa)
books.note: Classic work on the Prophet's character — English

school: Islamic Geography — served the Norman King Roger II of Sicily, producing the Tabula Rogeriana, the most accurate world map of the medieval era; it remained unsurpassed for three centuries

books.title: Nuzhat al-Mushtaq fi Ikhtiraq al-Afaq (The Book of Roger)
books.note: The Book of Roger (1154) — the most detailed world geography of the medieval age, with a map showing south at the top.

quotes: The earth is round like a sphere, and the waters cling to it naturally.
I have verified all I have written through my own travels and through the testimonies of those whose reliability I have confirmed.
The world is round as a sphere — and its waters are held in place by natural balance.
Geography is not merely the description of the earth — it is the mirror in which civilisation sees itself.
A people who do not know their land do not know themselves.

school: Shafi'i / Syrian Hadith School — his Tarikh Dimashq is the most comprehensive Islamic biographical dictionary

books.title: Tarikh Madinat Dimashq (History of Damascus — 80 volumes)
books.note: Most comprehensive Islamic biographical dictionary ever compiled — 80 volumes

school: Hanbali — Greatest medieval Islamic preacher; his sermons in Baghdad drew crowds of 100,000

books.title: Talbis Iblis (The Devil's Deception — critique of Sufi excess)
books.note: Famous Hanbali critique of esoteric Sufi practices — one of the most debated books in Islamic history.

books.title: Sifat al-Safwa (Descriptions of the Pure — Sufi biographies)

quotes: Time is your capital — do not spend it except on what will last.
The intelligent person does not waste a single moment — for moments are the capital of his life.
Lower your eyes from what is forbidden and your heart will be illuminated.

school: Ayyubid Sultan — recaptured Jerusalem in 1187 ending 88 years of Crusader rule; known for chivalry even to his enemies; Richard I of England called him 'the greatest king in the world'

books.title: Epistles of Saladin
books.url: ,note:"His correspondence with Crusader leaders — model of Islamic chivalry and diplomacy

quotes: Beware of bloodshed — never trust in spilled blood, for spilled blood never sleeps.
The strength of a ruler lies not in conquering enemies, but in conquering his own desires.
I warn you against shedding blood without justice — blood that is shed never sleeps.
It is not the walls of Jerusalem that make it holy — it is the hearts of those who love it.
The greatest victory I seek is not conquest of lands but conquest of my own self.

school: Maliki school — his travel account of the Hajj and Islamic world is the first great Islamic travel narrative

books.title: Rihlat Ibn Jubayr
books.url: https://archive.org/details/TravelsOfIbnJubayr",note:"The Travels — first great Islamic travel narrative; vivid description of Saladin's Egypt and the Crusader states

books.title: Hadiqat al-Haqiqa (Garden of Truth)
books.url: ,note:"First Sufi masnavi epic — Rumi and Attar both named Sanai as their master

books.title: Wikipedia — Ibn Jubayr
books.note: Wikipedia article

quotes: Travel is a foretaste of paradise for those with pure hearts.

school: Hanbali school — the greatest medieval Hanbali jurist; his al-Mughni is the definitive Hanbali legal reference

books.title: Al-Mughni
books.url: ,note:"10-volume Hanbali legal encyclopedia — still the primary Hanbali reference

quotes: Whoever occupies himself with what does not concern him misses what does concern him.

school: Ash'ari / Avicennian Theology — greatest medieval Islamic encyclopaedist; debated across Central Asia

books.title: Mafatih al-Ghayb (The Great Tafsir — 32 vols)
books.note: Most encyclopaedic Quranic commentary ever written — covers theology, philosophy, medicine and natural science.

quotes: The greater the difficulty, the more glory in surmounting it.
The end result of human intellect is bewilderment — and the end result of bewilderment is silence.
A scholar who abandons his knowledge for the sake of worldly reward has sold his soul at the cheapest price.

school: A thirteenth-century Kurdish historian from Mosul, he authored al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh (The Complete History), one of the most comprehensive universal chronicles in Arabic historiography. The work covers history from the creation to 1231 CE and is particularly valued for its detailed accounts of the Crusades and the Mongol invasions from a Muslim perspective. He also compiled Usd al-Ghaba, a major biographical dictionary of the Companions of the Prophet.
quotes: History does not repeat itself — but men repeat themselves, and so history appears to repeat.
The chronicler who flatters the powerful has betrayed his craft.

school: A thirteenth-century geographer of Greek origin who was enslaved as a child and later freed, he compiled Mu'jam al-Buldan (Dictionary of Countries), the most comprehensive geographical encyclopaedia of the medieval Islamic world. The work contains detailed entries on thousands of places, combining geographical data with historical, literary, and biographical information. He completed much of his writing while fleeing the Mongol invasions, preserving knowledge of cities and libraries that were subsequently destroyed.
quotes: The earth is vast, and every corner of it contains a lesson for those who travel with open eyes.
The city is not its walls — it is its scholars, its markets, and its memory.
I was a slave who was freed by learning — for knowledge makes every man free.

school: Ilkhanid Court — most widely read Islamic cosmographer; his encyclopedia was copied and illustrated for centuries

books.title: Aja'ib al-Makhluqat wa Ghara'ib al-Mawjudat (Wonders of Creation)
books.note: Most widely read Islamic encyclopedia of the medieval world — richly illustrated with cosmography, zoology, and geography

school: Akbarian — stepson and greatest student of Ibn Arabi; systematised Akbarian metaphysics; corresponded with Nasir al-Din al-Tusi

books.title: Miftah al-Ghayb
books.url: ,note:"Key to the Unseen — systematic exposition of Ibn Arabi's metaphysics

school: Ayyubid-Mamluk Court — his Mufarrij al-Kurub is primary Arabic source for Crusader-era history

books.title: Mufarrij al-Kurub fi Akhbar Bani Ayyub (Dispeller of Anxieties)
books.note: Primary Arabic source for Crusader-era history; author was also sent as diplomat to France's King Louis IX

school: Shafi'i School — Chief Judge of Syria; his biographical dictionary set the standard for Islamic biography

books.title: Wafayat al-A'yan wa Anba' Abna' al-Zaman (Deaths of Notables)
books.note: Greatest medieval Islamic biographical dictionary — 865 detailed entries

school: Maliki School
quotes: The Quran was not revealed to be placed on shelves — it was revealed to be acted upon.
The scholar who acts on his knowledge is like the sun — it gives light to others while burning itself.

school: Shadhiliyya Order (Cairo) / Al-Azhar

books.title: Al-Hikam al-Ata'iyya (Book of Wisdom — 264 aphorisms)
books.note: Most memorised and commented-upon text in Islamic mysticism after the Quran.

books.title: The Book of Wisdom (Kitab al-Hikam)
books.note: 264 Sufi aphorisms; widely studied Shadhili text — English

quotes: Do not seek His company through your own company with yourself — seek it by your fleeing from yourself to Him.

school: Twelver Shia jurisprudence — the most prolific medieval Shia scholar
Akbarian school — first Ottoman Shaykh al-Islam; brought Ibn Arabi to the Ottoman tradition

school: A fourteenth-century Mamluk-era scholar and biographer, he compiled al-Wafi bi-l-Wafayat (The Complete in Recording Deaths), one of the largest biographical dictionaries in Arabic literature, containing over fourteen thousand entries. He served in various administrative positions across Syria and Egypt while maintaining a prodigious scholarly output. His biographical method combined historical rigour with literary flair, preserving information about scholars, poets, and officials across the Islamic world.
quotes: The biographer who omits the flaws of his subject has not written a biography — he has written flattery.

school: A scholar of Arabic linguistics and literary theory active in the eleventh century, he authored two foundational works on Arabic rhetoric: Dala'il al-I'jaz (Proofs of Inimitability) and Asrar al-Balagha (Secrets of Eloquence). These treatises developed a sophisticated theory of meaning and composition that shaped Arabic literary criticism for centuries. His work on the inimitability of the Quran connected linguistics to theological discourse in innovative ways.
quotes: The science of rhetoric is the science of the heart of language.

school: A fifteenth-century Egyptian historian and one of the most prolific writers of the Mamluk period, he authored over two hundred works on Egyptian history, topography, and administration. His economic writings analysed famines and currency crises with a sophistication remarkable for his era.

school: A fifteenth-century Persian historian who lived in Herat under Timurid patronage, he authored Rawdat al-Safa (Garden of Purity), a universal history in Persian spanning from the creation to his own era. His accessible narrative style helped popularise historical knowledge among educated Persian-speaking audiences.

school: A Syrian intellectual and reformer of the late nineteenth century, he was one of the earliest Arab thinkers to articulate a vision of Arab nationalism and critique Ottoman despotism. He died in Cairo in 1902 under circumstances widely believed to have been poisoning by Ottoman agents.

school: An Egyptian jurist and intellectual of the late nineteenth and early twentieth century, he is widely regarded as one of the founders of the Arab feminist movement. His two landmark books, Tahrir al-Mar'a (The Liberation of Women, 1899) and al-Mar'a al-Jadida (The New Woman, 1900), argued for women's education, legal reform, and an end to forced veiling, sparking intense public debate across the Arab world. His ideas drew on both Islamic reformist thought and European liberal philosophy.

An Egyptian Islamic scholar and Sharia judge, he provoked one of the most significant intellectual controversies of the twentieth-century Arab world with his 1925 book al-Islam wa-Usul al-Hukm (Islam and the Foundations of Governance). The work argued that the caliphate had no basis in the Quran or Prophetic tradition and that Islam prescribed no specific form of government, a position that challenged both traditional religious authority and contemporary political movements seeking to restore the caliphate. He was stripped of his scholarly credentials by Al-Azhar but his thesis profoundly influenced subsequent debates on Islam and secularism.

Every Muslim is responsible for the state of his community — neutrality is not an option.

school: An Egyptian author, educator, and Islamist theorist who became the leading intellectual of the Muslim Brotherhood in the 1950s and 1960s. His prison writings, particularly Ma'alim fi al-Tariq (Milestones) and his multi-volume Quranic commentary Fi Zilal al-Quran (In the Shade of the Quran), articulated a radical critique of modern secular society and called for the establishment of an Islamic social order. He was executed by the Egyptian government in 1966, and his works have had a profound and contested influence on subsequent Islamist movements worldwide.

books.title: Fi Zilal al-Quran (In the Shade of the Quran)
In the Shade of the Quran (Fi Zilal al-Quran) Vol.1
books.note: Influential modern Quran commentary — English

quotes: The Quran is not a book of theology alone — it is a programme for the transformation of reality.
Ignorance is not simply a lack of knowledge — it is the state of a society that has rejected God’s sovereignty.
The Muslim who is content with injustice has not understood his faith.

school: Islamic Liberalism
Islamic international law pioneer — wrote the first French translation of the Quran; expert on early Islamic state

school: A twentieth-century South African Muslim missionary and orator of Indian Gujarati origin, he became internationally known for his public debates on comparative religion, particularly between Islam and Christianity. He founded the Islamic Propagation Centre International (IPCI) in Durban and authored numerous pamphlets and booklets on interfaith topics. His confrontational debating style and mastery of Biblical texts made him a polarising figure, admired by many Muslims for his da'wah efforts and criticised by others for his combative approach.
Islamic Modernism / Chicago School
quotes: The Quran must be understood in its historical context — and then applied to our own.
Islamic reform requires the courage to distinguish the eternal from the historical.
The greatest danger to Islam is not secularism — it is intellectual stagnation.
The Quran must be understood in its historical context — and then applied to our own.

The Islamisation of knowledge is not the rejection of modern learning — it is its reorientation.
Every discipline of knowledge must answer to the principle that there is no God but God.

quotes: Islamic reason must be emancipated from its own orthodoxies before it can engage the modern world.

school: A Syrian-American film director and producer, he is best known for directing The Message (1976), a major cinematic portrayal of the origins of Islam that was notable for not depicting the Prophet Muhammad on screen. He also directed Lion of the Desert (1981), depicting the Libyan resistance leader Omar Mukhtar's struggle against Italian colonial forces. His films brought Islamic and Arab history to international audiences and remain among the most widely viewed Islamic-themed productions.
Islamic Liberation Theology

Waiting is itself a form of action — if the waiting is conscious and prepared.
The intellectual who does not serve his people has sold his mind to those who oppress them.

school: A Tunisian historian and intellectual of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries, he was one of the foremost modern scholars of early Islamic history writing in both French and Arabic. His trilogy on the life of the Prophet Muhammad and the founding of Islam applied rigorous historical-critical methodology to early Islamic sources, earning both acclaim and controversy. He also wrote influential works on Islamic civilisation, Arab identity, and the crisis of modern Arab culture.
Hizmet (Service) Movement
quotes: Love is the most essential element of every religion.
Education is the most effective way to change the world.
Tolerance is the most precious human virtue — without it, coexistence is impossible.

school: Islamic Hermeneutics
quotes: The text is a human reading of the divine — and human readings can be wrong.
The Quran speaks to all ages — but each age must learn to listen in its own way.
Criticism of religious interpretation is not criticism of religion — it is its protection.

school: Iranian Religious Intellectualism
quotes: Religion is divine — religious knowledge is human, and therefore fallible.
The Quran is not a constitution to be applied literally — it is a vision to be interpreted wisely.
Pluralism is not relativism — it is the recognition that truth is larger than any one tradition’s grasp of it.

school: A British-Pakistani scholar, writer, and public intellectual, he is known for his prolific work on Islamic futures, science and Islam, and postcolonial criticism. His books, including Desperately Seeking Paradise and Reading the Qur'an, blend personal memoir with intellectual inquiry into the challenges facing contemporary Muslims. He has been a prominent voice calling for reform and critical thinking within Muslim communities while challenging Western stereotypes of Islam.

The most influential Shafi'i jurist after the Imam himself, Ibn Surayj systematised and propagated the Shafi'i school from Baghdad. Known as 'the second al-Shafi'i', he reportedly wrote approximately 400 works and trained a generation of jurists who would define Islamic legal theory. Al-Suyuti named him as the Mujaddid of the 3rd Islamic century.

books.title: al-Wada'i' li-Mansus al-Shara'i'
books.note: His major surviving work on Shafi'i legal theory and jurisprudence

books.title: al-Qiyas
books.note: Treatise on analogical reasoning in Islamic law

books.title: al-Radd 'ala Ibn Dawud
books.note: Refutation of the Zahiri school's positions

books.title: Sharh al-Mukhtasar (Commentary on al-Muzani's Epitome)
books.note: Key teaching text that trained a generation of Shafi'i jurists

school: A towering hadith scholar and jurist who served as Chief Judge of Egypt, Ibn Daqiq al-'Id mastered both the Shafi'i and Maliki schools. His Ihkam al-Ahkam sharh Umdat al-Ahkam is considered one of the finest commentaries on legal hadith. Al-Suyuti named him as the Mujaddid of the 7th Islamic century.

books.title: Ihkam al-Ahkam Sharh Umdat al-Ahkam
books.note: One of the finest commentaries on legal hadith ever written

books.title: al-Ilmam bi-Ahadith al-Ahkam
books.note: Collection of hadith relevant to legal rulings

books.title: al-Iqtirah fi Bayan al-Istilah
books.note: Important work on hadith terminology and methodology

books.title: Sharh al-Arbain al-Nawawiyya
books.note: Commentary on al-Nawawi's Forty Hadith

school: The undisputed head of the Shafi'i school in Baghdad during the late 10th and early 11th centuries, Abu Hamid al-Isfarayini's legal circles attracted up to 700 jurists. His student al-Mawardi became one of the most celebrated political theorists in Islamic history. Al-Suyuti named him as a Mujaddid of the 4th Islamic century.

books.title: Ta'liqah al-Kubra
books.note: His major compilation of Shafi'i jurisprudence, studied across the Islamic world

books.title: Kitab fi Usul al-Fiqh
books.note: Work on the principles of Islamic jurisprudence

school: The Mufti of Nishapur and a leading Shafi'i jurist of the 10th century, al-Su'luki was recognised alongside al-Baqillani as a co-Mujaddid of the 4th Islamic century. He combined expertise in jurisprudence with deep knowledge of hadith sciences.

books.title: Fatawa
books.note: Collection of his legal rulings as Mufti of Nishapur

books.title: Sharh al-Talkhis
books.note: Commentary on juristic methodology
